comments.author: niole
comments.body: Going to leave this as an object, because I think there is a scala test helper which you can use to mock objects `mockObject`

comments.author: ddl-grequeni
comments.body: I couldn't find any example of that in our code. Do you know how it manages to mock objects? AFAIK objects are like static methods in Java, so to mock them you need to tamper with bytecode, it's not a "legal" override of the real implementation. If we never did this before, I don't think we should introduce it to the code. But if we are not introducing that in this PR, I guess we'll just refactor this to a class when we need to mock it somewhere.

TBH, I think the documentation is not specific enough. It doesn't explicitly say that the domain is what's versioned. I think it's up to us how we want to organize the endpoints.

pr_title: DOM-45299 add endpoint for UI to call in order to implement hide project copy button

comments.author: ddl-grequeni
comments.body:
nit: Have you considered the alternative of returning a list of actions instead of an object?
It's more RESTful in the sense that `supported-actions` becomes a collection of supported actions. `copyProject` and `forkProject` boolean filters become an action type filter maybe? like `type=COPY` or `type=FORK` or no filter returns both. The implementation of such filter can be using an enum or sub-typing `SupportedActions`, not sure what's better, I would try the latter first.

I couldn't find examples of REST where an endpoint gives a single object, except for the `/collection/:id` cases. I'm not sure if it has some disadvantage to return an object under `/supported-actions` either, other than not following the "REST practice". Maybe extensibility? like the `/collection/:id` approach can be intuitively extended to other sub-resources of the actions if needed, but if `/supported-actions` gives an object, extending to sub-resources of the individual actions may be less natural. But these actions are unlikely to ever need sub-resources anyways.

BTW, I comment this here because this is going directly to the API. There is also the more boilerplate alternative of keeping whatever is useful in the code and then have a mapper in the API "presentation" layer (controller) to format it to the RESTful API dto that we want.

comments.author: niole
comments.body:
I like the mapping of action name to action better because it's more explicit and more convenient on the client side. The client consumer doesn't have to iterate through the results to get what they want, or combine them all into a map

Maybe we could think of `supported-actions` as a collection, but the response is just formatted as a map, not a list.

comments.author: niole
comments.body: I updated the query parameters to behave more like generic filtering parameters

comments.author: ddl-grequeni
comments.body:
The standard should be more strict in the definition of a collection, so that all APIs following it would return the same structure. The idea is that we return a collection of items in a certain format. Typically that is an array wrapped in some pagination details. Like
```
{totalCount: 10, paginationDetails: {...}, items: [ <the array of items> ] }
```
For the standard to be effective it should enforce this format, so that all Domino APIs will have the same format. If each team defines its own collection format, it will be a headache for consumers and it will not be standarized. Even when no pagination is needed, it should probably honor the format and just return null in the pagination details.

comments.author: niole
comments.body: This is unfortunately implemented randomly throughout the code base. `"api-server-utils/src/main/scala/domino/apiserverutils/pagination/PaginationFilter.scala` seems to have been intended to be the BP. It is however missing things like totalCount

pr_title: DOM-45299 add endpoint for UI to call in order to implement hide project copy button

comments.author: ddl-grequeni
comments.body: I think in order for the rate limit to work, we need `github` to be injected in a singleton scope (so all threads will share the same github API client instance). I guess it's thread safe, otherwise the rate limit config wouldn't make sense.

comments.author: niole
comments.body:
It looks like the github rate limits that we will be subject to is the "Primary rate limit for authenticated users": https://docs.github.com/en/rest/using-the-rest-api/rate-limits-for-the-rest-api?apiVersion=2022-11-28#primary-rate-limit-for-authenticated-users

and then secondary rate limit, which is about too many requests to individual endpoints.

I think we should be good if we add a singleton annotation here. We can't make repoman and nucleus-frontend us the same client because they are on separate pods.
